synopsis: A pilot stranded in the Sahara meets a small boy who has traveled from his distant asteroid, and through their conversations shares reflections on friendship, love, loss, and the folly of grown-up thinking. The story uses the prince's encounters with various inhabitants of different planets to critique vanity, authority, and the human tendency to overlook what truly matters.

sources.title: The Little Prince - Penguin Random House

synopsis: Christopher Chant discovers that his recurring dreams of traveling through a mysterious in-between place are real journeys to alternate worlds, and that he can bring objects back—drawing the attention of powerful enchanters who want him trained at Chrestomanci Castle. The novel traces his childhood as he learns the extent of his nine lives and his destiny as the next controller of the world's magic.

sources.title: The Lives of Christopher Chant - Wikipedia

synopsis: Rosemary Harper joins the crew of the Wayfarer, a tunneling ship that bores wormholes through space, and accompanies them on a long journey to a volatile destination at the edge of Galactic Commons territory. The novel focuses less on plot than on the lives and relationships of a diverse crew navigating both the cosmos and each other.

sources.title: The Long Way to a Small, Angry Planet - Wikipedia

synopsis: The book traces how the Federal Reserve's decade-long experiment with quantitative easing—flooding financial markets with cheap money—inflated asset prices, widened wealth inequality, and created fragility in the broader American economy. Using the story of Thomas Hoenig, a lone dissenting Fed official, Leonard argues that this monetary policy amounted to a slow-motion transfer of wealth to the already rich.

sources.title: The Lords of Easy Money - Simon & Schuster

synopsis: The final book of The Long Winter trilogy follows the survivors of humanity who have settled on the planet Eos, only to find that their predecessor colony has vanished without explanation and that buried artifacts on the planet's surface hold secrets about the grid and humanity's origins. James and Emma race to unravel the mystery while a new, unforeseen threat emerges against their fragile society.

Infinity Fowler is an elite bridger—a professional survival expert who protects tourists traveling to alternate versions of Earth—assigned to guide Desmond Weaver, a graduate student collecting evolutionary biology data on a radically different world. When the team arrives naked and unarmed on a planet swarming with lethal predators, their carefully made plans dissolve into a desperate struggle for survival.

sources.title: The Lure of Infinity - Stan C. Smith Official Site

synopsis: The book offers a visual and textual introduction to the molecular machines that operate inside living cells, using detailed illustrations to depict proteins, nucleic acids, and other biomolecules at work in processes such as metabolism, replication, and signaling. Written for non-specialists, it bridges biochemistry and molecular biology through imagery that conveys the scale and complexity of cellular life.

Rhodes chronicles the scientific, political, and human story behind the creation of the atomic bomb, from early twentieth-century discoveries about nuclear fission through the Manhattan Project and the bombings of Hiroshima and Nagasaki. The narrative traces the lives of the physicists and policymakers involved, examining how a scientific breakthrough became one of history's most consequential weapons.

sources.title: The Making of the Atomic Bomb - Simon & Schuster

synopsis: Mahon's biography examines the life and scientific work of James Clerk Maxwell, whose equations unifying electricity, magnetism, and light laid the foundation for much of modern physics and technology. The book traces Maxwell's career from his early mathematical insights through his development of electromagnetic theory, color photography, and statistical mechanics.

sources.title: The Man Who Changed Everything - Wiley

synopsis: Kanigel traces the life of Srinivasa Ramanujan, a self-taught Indian mathematician who grew up in poverty and, through a letter to Cambridge professor G. H. Hardy, entered one of history's most improbable scientific collaborations. The biography moves between the temples of Madras and the courts of Cambridge to explore how Ramanujan's intuitive genius produced results that mathematicians are still working to understand.

sources.title: The Man Who Knew Infinity - Simon & Schuster

synopsis: Drawing on his experience co-founding DoubleClick and other technology ventures, O'Connor presents a step-by-step framework for generating and commercializing new ideas, structured around five fundamentals: producing many viable ideas, selecting the right one, building a focused strategy, securing funding, and hiring talented people. The book uses real examples from the tech industry to illustrate how systematic thinking can replace luck in the innovation process.

Domingos surveys the five major schools of machine learning—symbolists, connectionists, evolutionaries, Bayesians, and analogizers—and argues that a single unified learning algorithm, the Master Algorithm, could in principle derive any knowledge from data. The book examines how each school draws on disciplines including neuroscience, statistics, and evolutionary biology, and considers what such a universal learner would mean for science, business, and society.

sources.title: The Master Algorithm - Hachette Book Group

synopsis: Wu examines a recurring pattern in the history of information industries—radio, telephone, film, television, and the internet—whereby each medium begins as an open, decentralized technology and eventually consolidates under the control of a dominant corporate power. The book uses this historical pattern to analyze the competitive strategies of contemporary technology companies and warn about the concentration of control over information flow.

McGilchrist argues across two volumes that Western civilization has become dominated by the brain's left hemisphere—which privileges narrow, instrumental thinking—at the expense of the right hemisphere's capacity for broad contextual awareness, leading to distorted views of consciousness, matter, and meaning. Drawing on neuroscience, philosophy, and the arts, the book proposes that restoring the right hemisphere's primacy is necessary to understand reality more completely.

Thomas collects twenty-nine brief essays that range across biology, medicine, and human behavior, exploring topics such as disease, death, cloning, the nature of mistakes, and the kinship between humans and other organisms. Written with wit and philosophical curiosity, the pieces use biological phenomena as lenses for reflecting on the broader conditions of human existence.

sources.title: The Medusa and the Snail - Penguin Random House

synopsis: Set in medieval Baghdad, a fabric merchant encounters an alchemist's gate capable of transporting people twenty years forward or backward in time, then hears the intertwined tales of others who have used it. Through nested narratives structured like the Arabian Nights, the novelette explores whether knowledge of the past or future can change one's fate.

sources.title: Merchant and the Alchemist's Gate - Subterranean Press

synopsis: Gregor Samsa wakes one morning to find himself transformed into a giant insect-like creature, and the story follows the deteriorating relationships between him and his family as they struggle to adapt to his new existence. Kafka's novella uses this fantastical premise to examine themes of alienation, economic dependency, guilt, and the ways identity can be destroyed by the needs and perceptions of others.

Nora Seed, at the lowest point of her life, finds herself in a mysterious library between life and death where each book allows her to inhabit an alternate version of her life based on different choices she could have made. As she moves between these possible lives—pursuing different careers, relationships, and paths—she must determine what would actually make her existence worth living.

sources.title: The Midnight Library - Matt Haig Official Site

synopsis: Moullakis and Wright trace Macquarie Bank's rise from a small Australian merchant bank founded in 1969 to a global infrastructure and asset management powerhouse, examining the strategic decisions, scandals, and management culture behind its growth. Drawing on interviews with past and present executives, the book explains the loose-tight risk philosophy that made Macquarie both distinctive and profitable.
